posts.title: Multi-agent systems are just pipelines
posts.hook:
Everyone's talking about multi-agent AI systems like they're some new paradigm.

They're not. They're ETL pipelines with better PR.
posts.body:
Everyone's talking about multi-agent AI systems like they're some new paradigm.

They're not. They're ETL pipelines with better PR.

I run a multi-agent system on a single Mac Mini. One agent writes blog posts. Another scouts Reddit threads. Another enriches leads through Clay and pushes them to Attio. Another generates daily dashboards.

They don't talk to each other in some magical orchestration layer. They write to files. They read from a SQLite database. They run on cron jobs.

The architecture that works isn't the one with the most sophisticated agent-to-agent communication protocol. It's the one where each agent does one thing well, writes its output somewhere predictable, and the next agent picks it up.

Sound familiar? That's because we solved this problem in data engineering a decade ago. Source --> transform --> load --> next step reads from the load.

The teams shipping real agent systems right now aren't debating frameworks. They're writing bash scripts that chain Claude CLI calls together with jq and sqlite3.

Sophistication isn't in the architecture. It's in knowing what to automate and what to keep manual.

Most of my "agents" are 40-line Python scripts with a system prompt and a cron schedule. They've been running for months without breaking.
posts.cta: What's the simplest agent system you've shipped that actually stuck?

remixes.label: plumbing-metaphor
remixes.hook:
The most overhyped term in AI right now is 'multi-agent orchestration.'

You know what orchestration actually looks like? A cron job that runs at midnight.
remixes.body:
The most overhyped term in AI right now is 'multi-agent orchestration.'

You know what orchestration actually looks like? A cron job that runs at midnight.

I have seven AI agents running in production. They write blog posts, scout Reddit, enrich leads, generate dashboards, and distribute content across platforms.

Not one of them knows the others exist.

There's no message bus. No shared memory layer. No graph of agent relationships pinned to a whiteboard. Each agent is a Python script with a single job. It reads from a file or database, does its thing, writes output somewhere. The next agent picks it up when its own cron fires.

This is plumbing. Boring, reliable plumbing.

The AI industry wants you to believe you need a framework to coordinate agents. LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen. These tools solve real problems for research teams exploring complex reasoning chains. But for 90% of production agent use cases, you're building a pipeline.

Pipelines don't need coordination protocols. They need clear inputs, predictable outputs, and error handling that doesn't cascade.

I built mine on launchd, SQLite, and JSON files. It runs on a Mac Mini that costs $0.00/month in cloud bills. When something breaks, I check a log file. Not a distributed tracing dashboard.

Stop architecting. Start plumbing.
remixes.cta: What's running your agents in production? Framework or cron job?

remixes.label: framework-graveyard
remixes.hook:
I've tried four multi-agent frameworks this year.

All four are uninstalled. The cron jobs are still running.
remixes.body:
I've tried four multi-agent frameworks this year.

All four are uninstalled. The cron jobs are still running.

Here's the pattern I kept hitting. Framework promises elegant agent coordination. I spend two days learning the abstraction layer. I wire up my agents. Something breaks in the framework's internal message passing. I debug the framework instead of my actual problem. I rip it out and go back to what worked.

What works: isolated scripts with clear contracts.

Agent 1 writes a JSON file. Agent 2 reads that JSON file. No handshake protocol. No shared state. No "agent memory" layer. Just files on disk.

The insight that changed how I build: agents don't need to collaborate in real time. They need to leave artifacts for each other. A blog post draft in a folder. An enriched lead record in SQLite. A content brief in markdown.

This is how Unix pipes work. This is how ETL works. This is how every reliable data system has worked for decades.

The multi-agent hype cycle wants you to believe your agents need to negotiate, delegate, and reason together. In practice, the most reliable agent systems are the ones where each agent is blissfully unaware of everything else.

Simple beats clever. Every time.
remixes.cta: Have you ripped out an agent framework and gone simpler? What did you replace it with?

tones.advisor:
If you're evaluating multi-agent frameworks right now, I'd encourage you to take a step back and audit what you actually need.

In my experience running production AI agents for months, the orchestration layer is almost never the bottleneck. The bottleneck is reliability. Can each agent do its job consistently? Can you debug it when it fails? Can you add a new agent without breaking existing ones?

Here's what I recommend: start with isolated scripts. One agent, one job, one cron schedule. Let them communicate through files or a simple database. SQLite is more than enough for most use cases.

I run seven agents on a single Mac Mini. Blog generation, lead enrichment, Reddit scouting, content distribution, daily dashboards. None of them share memory or coordinate in real time. Each one reads from a known location, processes, and writes output.

The result is a system I can debug by reading log files, extend by adding a new script, and monitor by checking output folders.

Before you adopt a framework, prove you need one. Build the pipeline first. Add coordination only when isolated agents genuinely can't solve the problem. In my experience, that moment comes much later than you'd expect. Often it never comes at all.
tones.provocateur:
The multi-agent framework industry is selling you a solution to a problem you don't have.

LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen. They're building increasingly complex abstractions on top of a simple truth: most agent workflows are just sequential scripts.

Agent writes thing. Next agent reads thing. Done.

But 'sequential scripts' doesn't get VC funding. 'Autonomous agent orchestration platform' does. So we get frameworks that add coordination layers, shared memory systems, and agent negotiation protocols to solve problems that exist in research papers, not production systems.

I run seven production agents. They generate real revenue. They handle real content pipelines and lead enrichment workflows. The total infrastructure cost is a Mac Mini I already owned and some Python scripts triggered by cron.

No Kubernetes. No message queue. No vector database for 'agent memory.' No graph visualization of agent relationships.

You know what my agents share? A SQLite database and some JSON files on disk. That's the entire 'orchestration layer.'

 is that multi-agent systems are boring when they work. And the industry can't sell boring. So they make it complicated, package the complexity, and charge you for the privilege of debugging their abstraction instead of your actual business logic.

posts.title: Outbound is dead, long live outbound
posts.hook:
Outbound isn't dead. Your outbound is dead.

Big difference.
posts.body:
Outbound isn't dead. Your outbound is dead.

Big difference.

I sent 200+ cold emails a day as an SDR. Primary domain. No warmup tool. SalesLoft sequences with zero personalization infrastructure. We just... sent them. And it worked, because the bar was low and inboxes weren't war zones.

That version of outbound is absolutely dead.

What replaced it is an engineering problem, not a sales problem. Here's what a modern outbound motion actually looks like:

🔧 Clay pulls firmographic + technographic signals
🔧 AI scores intent against your ICP definition
🔧 Enrichment layers stack (Apollo for contacts, Exa for content signals, job postings for timing)
🔧 Sequences route through warmed infrastructure with domain rotation
🔧 Reply handling feeds back into scoring

The SDRs who thrive now aren't the ones who can send more. They're the ones who understand why a lead scored high and can write the 3 sentences that land because of that context.

RevOps teams building this as an engineering system are seeing 3-4x reply rates compared to teams still running the 2019 playbook of "buy a list, load it, blast it."

The skillset shifted. The opportunity didn't shrink. It just moved upstream to the people who can build the system.
posts.cta: If you're still running outbound like it's 2019, what's blocking the rebuild?

inspired_by.author: Ankur Nagpal
inspired_by.summary: Outlines a revops playbook for B2B SaaS, focusing on outbound automation tools like Apollo and Salesloft. Details how sales ops can sync with content strategy to boost pipeline velocity. Includes case study from scaling ARR 3x.

remixes.label: engineering-takeover
remixes.hook:
The best outbound teams I know don't have SDRs anymore.

They have engineers.
remixes.body:
The best outbound teams I know don't have SDRs anymore.

They have engineers.

This isn't some futurist prediction. I lived the transition. I was the SDR sending 200 cold emails a day from a primary domain with zero personalization. SalesLoft sequences, generic templates, pray for a 2% reply rate.

That playbook died because inboxes evolved faster than sales teams did. Spam filters got smarter. Buyers got more skeptical. The volume play stopped working.

What replaced it looks nothing like sales. It looks like software engineering.

Domain rotation across 15+ sending domains with independent warmup schedules. AI-generated personalization that references the prospect's actual tech stack, job postings, and recent company news. Deliverability monitoring that tracks inbox placement rates, not just open rates.

I built this pipeline with Clay for enrichment, Lemlist for sending, Attio for CRM, and custom scripts connecting them. The whole system runs autonomously. I set targeting criteria, the pipeline finds accounts, enriches contacts, personalizes messages, and sends them on a schedule.

The person who builds this isn't an SDR. They're an engineer who understands revenue. And that's the skill gap nobody's talking about. The future of outbound belongs to people who can build the machine, not just operate it.
remixes.cta: Is your outbound team hiring engineers yet? Or still hiring SDRs and hoping the old playbook works?

remixes.label: autopsy-report
remixes.hook:
I want to do a quick autopsy on every outbound campaign that failed in 2025.

Cause of death: the same in almost every case.
remixes.body:
I want to do a quick autopsy on every outbound campaign that failed in 2025.

Cause of death: the same in almost every case.

Not bad messaging. Not wrong ICP. Not poor timing. Infrastructure.

Sending from a primary domain with no warmup. One domain, one mailbox, 200 emails a day. Google flags it in a week. Your entire company email reputation tanks. Now your CEO's emails to actual clients are landing in spam.

No enrichment layer. You're sending to job titles, not people. You don't know their tech stack, their hiring patterns, their recent funding round. So your 'personalization' is their first name and company in a template.

No deliverability monitoring. You're tracking open rates, which are increasingly unreliable, and reply rates, which are a lagging indicator. By the time reply rates drop, your domain is already burned.

I rebuilt outbound from scratch as an engineering system. Dedicated sending infrastructure with domain rotation. Enrichment pipelines that pull from multiple data sources before a single email goes out. Real-time deliverability scoring.

The result: consistently landing in primary inboxes with messages that reference things the prospect actually cares about.

Outbound isn't dead. But the version most teams are running should be.
remixes.cta: Run a deliverability audit on your outbound this week. What did you find?

tones.advisor:
If you're running outbound in 2026 the same way you did in 2020, here's the honest assessment: you're burning money and domain reputation simultaneously.

I've been on both sides of this. I was the SDR sending 200+ cold emails a day from a primary domain. No warmup. No enrichment. SalesLoft sequences with templates that had first name and company name merged in. It worked because the bar was low.

That bar has moved. Here's what I'd recommend.

First, treat your sending infrastructure as a product. Dedicated domains, proper warmup schedules, rotation logic. This is engineering work, not sales ops work.

Second, build an enrichment layer before you build sequences. You need to know what someone's company is actually doing before you write to them. Clay, Apollo, LinkedIn Sales Nav. Stack these data sources and build composite profiles.

Third, monitor deliverability as a real-time metric. Inbox placement rate matters more than open rate. If you're not tracking where your emails actually land, you're flying blind.

The teams winning at outbound right now have rebuilt it from the infrastructure up. They treat it as a technical system with engineering requirements, not a sales activity with activity quotas.

This shift is happening whether sales leaders acknowledge it or not. Better to lead it than get caught behind it.
tones.provocateur:
Here's what nobody in sales leadership wants to admit.

The SDR role as it existed for the last decade was an arbitrage play on cheap labor and unsophisticated spam filters. That's it. That's the whole strategy. Hire 22-year-olds, give them a SalesLoft login, tell them to hit 200 activities a day, and pray.

I know because I was one of those 22-year-olds. I sent the emails. I hit the quotas. And it worked, because in 2019 you could blast a primary domain with generic templates and get meetings.

That version of outbound didn't die because of AI. It died because email providers got smarter and buyers got exhausted. Google's spam detection alone killed half the playbook.

So what now? The companies still winning at outbound did something uncomfortable. They fired the SDR team and hired engineers. Or they turned their SDRs into technical operators who can manage enrichment pipelines, deliverability infrastructure, and AI personalization systems.

I built a system that does the work of a 3-person SDR team. Domain rotation, AI enrichment, automated personalization, deliverability monitoring. It runs on cron jobs. It doesn't call in sick. It doesn't need ramping.

The outbound isn't dead crowd is right. But they're selling you the old version while quietly rebuilding everything behind the scenes.

posts.title: Solo devs shipping faster than funded teams
posts.hook:
A solo developer with Claude Code and a $20/month hosting bill is now outshipping 5-person engineering teams.

This isn't a hot take. I'm watching it happen in real time.
posts.body:
A solo developer with Claude Code and a $20/month hosting bill is now outshipping 5-person engineering teams.

This isn't a hot take. I'm watching it happen in real time.

I run a monorepo with 3 Next.js sites, a mission control app, an AI chat system, automated content pipelines, and a full ABM engine. One person. One Mac Mini.

The unlock wasn't some breakthrough in my coding ability. It was that the bottleneck moved. Used to be: "can you write this code?" Now it's: "do you know what to build and why?"

That second question is where former operators crush it. People who've sat in the sales seat, run campaigns, felt the pain of bad tooling. They know exactly what's broken because they lived inside the broken thing for years.

The Pieter Levels model works not because solo development got easier (it did), but because taste and context became the scarce resource. AI can write the code. It can't tell you which code matters.

Funded teams still win on scale, compliance, enterprise sales motions. But for 0-to-1 products? The solo builder with domain expertise and AI tooling is the most dangerous competitor in the market right now.

And most of them are ex-operators, not ex-engineers.
posts.cta: Operators turning builders... who else is seeing this pattern?

inspired_by.summary: Pieter shared his journey of shipping multiple products as a solo developer, including launching a new AI tool in under a week. He emphasized the importance of building in public to gain feedback and users quickly. The post details his revenue updates and lessons from rapid iteration.

remixes.label: leverage-shift
remixes.hook:
The biggest lie in tech hiring right now: 'We need to scale the team to ship faster.'

No you don't. You need one person who knows how to use the new tools.
remixes.body:
The biggest lie in tech hiring right now: 'We need to scale the team to ship faster.'

No you don't. You need one person who knows how to use the new tools.

I'm watching this play out in real time from both sides. Funded startups with 5-person engineering teams shipping quarterly. Solo operators with AI-assisted development shipping weekly.

The difference isn't talent. The funded teams often have better engineers. The difference is leverage.

A 5-person team has standups, sprint planning, code reviews, architectural debates, Jira ticket grooming, and deployment approvals. Each person might write code for 3 hours a day. Multiply by 5 and you get 15 hours of actual development per day across the whole team.

A solo developer with Claude Code skips every one of those coordination costs. No meetings. No tickets. No waiting for reviews. See a problem, fix it, ship it. I regularly push 5-10 meaningful commits per day across multiple projects.

My stack right now: 3 Next.js sites, a mission control dashboard, an AI chat system, automated content pipelines, and a full ABM lead engine. All running on a single Mac Mini. All maintained by one person.

The coordination tax of teams used to be worth paying because individual output was limited. AI tools changed that equation. One person's output ceiling just went up by 5x. The coordination cost stayed the same.

That math doesn't work out well for large teams doing work that one person could own.
remixes.cta: How many people on your team are there for coordination, not output?

remixes.label: operator-to-builder
remixes.hook:
I'm not a developer by training. I'm a GTM operator who learned to code because AI made the gap crossable.

That's the real story nobody's talking about.
remixes.body:
I'm not a developer by training. I'm a GTM operator who learned to code because AI made the gap crossable.

That's the real story nobody's talking about.

Six months ago, I couldn't have built a Next.js site from scratch. I understood systems, data flows, and automation logic from years of GTM ops work. But translating that into production code was a wall I couldn't climb alone.

Claude Code removed the wall. Not by writing code for me. By making the gap between 'I know what this should do' and 'I can make it do that' small enough to cross.

Now I run a monorepo with three websites, an AI chat system, automated content pipelines, CRM integrations, and a lead enrichment engine. I'm not copying and pasting AI output. I'm building real systems, debugging real problems, reading real stack traces.

The traditional path was: learn to code for 2 years, then build your ideas. The new path is: describe what you need, understand the code that comes back, iterate on it, learn by shipping.

I learned more about TypeScript in 3 months of building with Claude Code than I would have in a year of tutorials. Because I was solving real problems with real stakes, not contrived exercises.

This is happening everywhere. Marketers building their own dashboards. Sales ops people building their own integrations. Product managers prototyping features instead of writing specs.

The operator-to-builder pipeline is the most underrated trend in tech right now.
remixes.cta: Who else crossed from operator to builder? What was the tool that made it possible?

If you're leading an engineering team right now, I'd encourage you to honestly assess where your time goes.

In my experience, the bottleneck for most teams isn't engineering talent. It's coordination overhead. Standups, sprint planning, code reviews, architectural discussions, deployment processes. Each one is reasonable in isolation. Together, they eat 40-60% of your team's available hours.

Here's what's changed. AI-assisted development tools like Claude Code have dramatically increased individual output capacity. A single developer can now realistically maintain the kind of system that used to require a team of 3-5.

I'm living proof. I run a monorepo with 3 Next.js sites, a mission control app, an AI chat system, automated content pipelines, and a full ABM engine. One person. One Mac Mini.

My recommendation for teams: before your next hire, run an experiment. Take your best engineer, remove them from all meetings for two weeks, give them Claude Code or Cursor, and measure their output. Compare that to the team's collective output during the same period.

The results will tell you whether you need more people or fewer coordination costs.

This doesn't mean teams are dead. Complex systems with regulatory requirements, security concerns, and massive scale still need teams. But for a surprising amount of software work, the solo-plus-AI model ships faster and more reliably than a coordinated team.
tones.provocateur:
Five-person engineering teams are a liability now and most CTOs are pretending they don't see it.

Here's the math nobody wants to do out loud. Five engineers at $180k average salary: $900k/year. Factor in benefits, equipment, office space, management overhead: call it $1.2M. Those five engineers have standups, sprint planning, code reviews, architectural debates. Actual coding time per person: maybe 3 hours a day. Total productive hours: 15 per day across the whole team.

One solo developer with Claude Code and a Max subscription: $200/month for the AI, $20/month for hosting. Coding time per day: 8-10 hours. No coordination tax. No waiting for reviews. No meetings about meetings.

I'm building what used to be a team's worth of infrastructure. Three websites, AI systems, content automation, lead pipelines, CRM integrations. All shipping on a Mac Mini.

The response I keep hearing: 'But code quality! But reliability! But you'll burn out!'

My code has fewer bugs than most team projects I've worked on. Because there's one person making decisions, one mental model of the system, and zero communication gaps where bugs hide.

The uncomfortable reality: most engineering teams exist because of inertia, not necessity. We hired teams because individual output had a ceiling. AI tools removed that ceiling. The teams are still there because firing people is hard and admitting you over-hired is harder.

posts.title: AI agent infrastructure is overthought
posts.hook:
Your AI agent doesn't need Kubernetes.

It needs a cron job and a JSON file.
posts.body:
Your AI agent doesn't need Kubernetes.

It needs a cron job and a JSON file.

I've been running production AI agents for months. Not proofs of concept. Actual systems that generate content, enrich leads, scout engagement opportunities, and post to multiple platforms autonomously.

The infrastructure:
- launchd plists (macOS cron equivalent)
- Python scripts that call Claude's API
- SQLite for state
- JSON files for inter-agent communication
- A single Mac Mini that never sleeps

Total infrastructure cost beyond the API calls: $0.

remixes.label: anti-complexity
remixes.hook:
I keep seeing AI agent architecture diagrams with 15 boxes and 30 arrows.

My production system has one box. It says 'cron job.'
remixes.body:
I keep seeing AI agent architecture diagrams with 15 boxes and 30 arrows.

My production system has one box. It says 'cron job.'

Every week someone posts a new agent framework with a beautiful diagram showing agents communicating through message queues, sharing vector databases, and coordinating through some orchestration layer. Every week I look at my own system and wonder if I'm doing something wrong.

Then I check my logs. Everything ran. Content got published. Leads got enriched. Dashboards got generated. No failures.

My infrastructure is embarrassingly simple. launchd plists that fire Python scripts on a schedule. Those scripts call Claude's API, process the response, and write output to SQLite or JSON files. That's it.

No Docker containers. No message queues. No vector databases. No Kubernetes clusters. No microservices. No API gateway. No load balancer.

A Mac Mini sitting on my desk running macOS.

The reason this works is that most agent tasks are fundamentally simple. Read some input. Process it with an LLM. Write the output somewhere. The complexity people add is almost never about the problem. It's about anticipated future problems that never materialize.

I've been running this setup for months. The only infrastructure incident I've had was when my Mac Mini's power cord came loose.

Boring infrastructure is a feature, not a limitation.
remixes.cta: What's the most embarrassingly simple infrastructure running your most reliable system?

remixes.label: cost-breakdown
remixes.hook:
People ask me what my AI agent infrastructure costs.

I did the math. It's less than my coffee budget.
remixes.body:
People ask me what my AI agent infrastructure costs.

I did the math. It's less than my coffee budget.

Here's the actual breakdown of my production AI agent system.

Hardware: Mac Mini I already owned. Incremental cost: $0/month. It sits on my desk and runs 24/7.

Compute: launchd cron jobs triggering Python scripts. No cloud instances. No serverless functions. No container orchestration. Cost: $0/month.

AI API calls: Claude API for content generation, lead enrichment, and analysis. This is the real cost. Running multiple agents daily, including a blog generator, Reddit scout, lead enricher, and dashboard generator. Monthly API spend: roughly $50-80.

Database: SQLite. Free. Stores everything from initiative tracking to XP systems to daily cost metrics. Single file on disk.

Hosting for websites: Cloudflare Pages. Free tier covers everything.

Total monthly cost for a system that would have required a small team and significant cloud infrastructure two years ago: under $100.

The reason this number is so low isn't clever optimization. It's architectural simplicity. When you don't have Kubernetes, you don't pay for Kubernetes. When you don't have a message queue, you don't pay for a message queue. When you don't have a vector database, you don't pay for a vector database.

Every piece of infrastructure you add is a cost multiplier. The cheapest infrastructure is the infrastructure you don't need.
remixes.cta: What's your actual monthly cost for running AI agents in production? Not projected. Actual.

posts.title: Enterprise AI agents fail at deployment not design
posts.hook:
Every enterprise AI agent demo I've seen looks incredible.

Every enterprise AI agent deployment I've seen looks like a disaster.
posts.body:
Every enterprise AI agent demo I've seen looks incredible.

Every enterprise AI agent deployment I've seen looks like a disaster.

The gap isn't the model. It's the 47 things between the model and production that nobody talks about on stage.

Authentication. Your agent needs to log into Salesforce, pull from HubSpot, write to Slack, update Jira. Each one has its own auth flow, rate limits, and failure modes. Your demo used hardcoded API keys. Your production needs OAuth refresh tokens that expire at the worst possible moment.

State management. The demo was stateless. Production agents need to remember what they did yesterday, what's in progress, what failed and needs retry. That's a database design problem, not an AI problem.

Error handling. In the demo, every API call succeeded. In production, Salesforce returns a 500 on the third call of a batch because one record had a validation rule from 2019 that nobody remembers creating.

Permissions. Your agent can do things faster than a human. That's terrifying when it has write access to your CRM and the guardrails are "we'll add those later."

The teams winning at enterprise AI agents aren't the ones with the best prompts. They're the ones who've done enough integrations to know where things break.

Infrastructure experience is the moat. Not model selection.
posts.cta: What's the ugliest integration problem you've hit deploying an AI agent?

inspired_by.author: Amanda Scalzi
inspired_by.summary: Scalzi outlines best practices for deploying AI agents in enterprise automation, addressing pain points in current infrastructure stacks. She highlights emerging developer tools that simplify agent integration and monitoring. Provides a framework for measuring ROI on AI investments.

remixes.label: 47-things-list
remixes.hook:
I tried to list every problem between 'AI agent works in demo' and 'AI agent works in production.'

I stopped counting at 47.
remixes.body:
I tried to list every problem between 'AI agent works in demo' and 'AI agent works in production.'

I stopped counting at 47.

Here are just the ones I've personally hit.

Authentication: your agent needs credentials for Salesforce, HubSpot, Slack, Jira, Gmail, and your data warehouse. Each has its own OAuth flow. Each expires on a different schedule. Each has different rate limits. One token expiration at 2 AM and your entire pipeline stalls.

Data format chaos: the same field means different things in different systems. 'Company name' in HubSpot doesn't match 'Account name' in Salesforce. Phone numbers come in 15 different formats. Dates are sometimes strings, sometimes timestamps, sometimes ISO 8601, sometimes whatever format a junior developer felt like using in 2019.

Error handling: LLMs fail in ways traditional software doesn't. The API returns a 200 but the output is garbage. The model hallucinates a field that doesn't exist. The response is valid JSON but the schema changed. You can't just catch exceptions. You need semantic validation on every output.

Permissions: your agent needs write access to production CRM data. Who approves that? What happens when it writes bad data? How do you roll back 500 incorrect contact updates?

Monitoring: traditional APM tools don't capture LLM-specific failures. You need to track token usage, response quality, and output drift alongside standard metrics.

Every demo skips all of this. That's why demos look incredible and deployments look like disasters.
remixes.cta: What's the problem between demo and production that bit you hardest?

remixes.label: demo-to-deploy-gap
remixes.hook:
Conference stage: 'Watch our AI agent book a meeting in 30 seconds!'

Reality: 'Watch our AI agent fail silently for 3 weeks because the OAuth token expired.'
remixes.body:
Conference stage: 'Watch our AI agent book a meeting in 30 seconds!'

Reality: 'Watch our AI agent fail silently for 3 weeks because the OAuth token expired.'

I've been building production AI agents for months. Not demos. Not prototypes. Systems that run every day, handle real data, and break in real ways.

The gap between demo and production isn't the AI. The AI part is the easy part. You call an API, you get a response, you parse it. That takes an afternoon to build.

The hard part is everything around the AI.

It's the CRM that rate-limits you after 100 API calls. It's the enrichment provider that returns different JSON schemas for different record types. It's the email system that silently drops messages when your domain reputation dips. It's the database migration that breaks your agent's expected column names.

The hardest problem I solved wasn't prompt engineering. It was building a retry system that handles partial failures gracefully. When your agent enriches 200 leads and 3 of them fail, you can't just rerun the whole batch. You need to track which ones succeeded, skip them on retry, and handle the failures individually.

This is systems engineering. It's the same class of problems we've been solving in backend development for decades. But the AI industry presents it as a new frontier because they're selling the model, not the deployment.

The model works. It's worked for a while now. The deployment is where careers are made or broken.
remixes.cta: What percentage of your agent project timeline was the AI part vs. everything else?

tones.advisor:
If you're planning to deploy AI agents in an enterprise environment, I'd recommend budgeting 80% of your timeline for integration work, not AI development.

The model selection, prompt engineering, and agent logic will take about 20% of the project. Everything else is systems integration, and it's where most deployments stall.

Here's what to plan for.

Authentication management: every system your agent touches has its own auth flow, token lifecycle, and rate limits. Build a credential management layer early. Treat it as infrastructure, not an afterthought.

Data normalization: your agent will read from and write to systems that use different schemas, formats, and conventions. Build transformation layers between each integration. Don't assume field names or data types will be consistent.

Error handling for LLM outputs: traditional try/catch won't cover it. You need semantic validation. The API can return a 200 with output that's technically valid but functionally wrong. Build validation that checks meaning, not just structure.

Rollback capability: when your agent writes bad data to production systems (it will), you need the ability to identify affected records and reverse the changes. Design for this from day one.

Monitoring: standard APM isn't sufficient. Track token usage, output quality metrics, and behavioral drift alongside traditional application metrics.

The companies that deploy agents successfully are the ones that treat the project as a systems integration challenge, not an AI challenge.
tones.provocateur:
Every AI agent demo is a lie by omission.

Not intentionally. The people presenting genuinely believe in what they built. But they're showing you the 5% of the work that's interesting and hiding the 95% that's painful.

Watch any conference demo. The agent queries a database, summarizes results, and sends a Slack message. Crowd goes wild. Standing ovation. Series B secured.

Nobody asks: what happens when the Slack API rate limits you? What happens when the database schema changes? What happens when the OAuth token expires at 3 AM? What happens when the model hallucinates a column name that doesn't exist? What happens when your agent writes incorrect data to 500 CRM records and nobody notices for a week?

I've hit every single one of these in production. The authentication problem alone took longer to solve than the entire agent logic. Building retry systems for partial batch failures was a week of work. Monitoring LLM output quality is an ongoing project with no finish line.

The AI industry has a demo problem. They've convinced enterprises that deploying an agent is like installing software. Download, configure, go. In reality, it's more like building a custom integration for every system in your stack, simultaneously, with a non-deterministic component in the middle.

The model is the easy part. It's been the easy part for over a year. Everything else is where the real work lives.

posts.title: Content strategy is a GTM engineering problem
posts.hook:
Your content strategy isn't a marketing problem.

It's a data pipeline problem wearing a marketing hat.
posts.body:
Your content strategy isn't a marketing problem.

It's a data pipeline problem wearing a marketing hat.

I automated my entire content pipeline. Not the writing (that's still me plus Claude as a co-pilot). The orchestration.

Every morning, scripts scout Reddit, LinkedIn, and X for trending conversations in my space. They score relevance against my positioning pillars. They surface the top opportunities with context about why they're worth engaging.

Every night, a cron job generates a daily blog post from accumulated context. Another job tracks which keywords I've covered, which are trending, and where the gaps are.

The content itself has to be authentic. The system around it doesn't.

April Dunford is right that positioning drives content strategy. But positioning without distribution infrastructure is just a good idea sitting in a Google Doc.

The play:

✅ Define 3-5 positioning pillars
✅ Build signal detection for each pillar (keyword tracking, competitor monitoring, community scanning)
✅ Automate the scout layer so opportunities surface to you
✅ Keep the creative layer human
✅ Automate distribution across platforms from a single source

One piece of content should touch 4+ platforms with platform-native formatting. That's not a creative problem. That's an engineering problem.

The teams treating content like a factory (in the best way) are producing 5x the output with better consistency.
posts.cta: How much of your content workflow is still fully manual?

remixes.label: systems-thinking-breakdown
remixes.hook:
Most founders treat content like a creative exercise.

The ones winning treat it like infrastructure.
remixes.body:
Most founders treat content like a creative exercise.

The ones winning treat it like infrastructure.

Think about how you treat your sales pipeline. There's a CRM. There's scoring. There's stages, automation, alerts when deals stall. Nobody runs sales on vibes and a Google Doc.

But content? Most companies run it on a shared spreadsheet, a Slack channel called #content-ideas, and whoever remembered to post this week.

I rebuilt my content operation as an engineering system. Not because I'm a perfectionist. Because I was dropping balls.

The stack: Python scripts that scan Reddit, LinkedIn, and X every morning for conversations in my space. A scoring function that ranks opportunities against my three positioning pillars. A tracker that logs what I posted, where, and what performed. A brief generator that hands me the top 5 angles before I write a single word.

The writing is still mine. The strategy is still mine. But the scouting, scheduling, tracking, and distribution? That's code now.

Result: I went from posting when I felt inspired (maybe twice a week) to publishing daily across four platforms with better topic selection than when I was doing it manually.

Content isn't creative work that happens to need distribution. It's a system that happens to need a human voice at the center.
remixes.cta: What part of your content workflow would you automate first if you could?

remixes.label: before-after-pipeline
remixes.hook:
6 months ago I spent 4 hours a day on content.

Now I spend 45 minutes and publish more.
remixes.body:
6 months ago I spent 4 hours a day on content.

Now I spend 45 minutes and publish more.

The difference wasn't writing faster. It was eliminating everything around the writing that wasn't actually writing.

Here's what my mornings used to look like: scroll LinkedIn for 30 minutes looking for trends. Check Reddit for 20 minutes. Open a blank doc. Stare at it. Pick a topic based on gut feel. Write something. Manually cross-post to three platforms. Forget to track performance.

Here's what they look like now: open my terminal. A script already ran at 6am. It pulled trending posts from my target communities, scored them against my positioning, and generated a brief with the top angles. I pick one, write the post with Claude as a sounding board, and hit a command that formats and queues it across platforms.

The writing quality is better because I'm not fatigued from the busywork before I start. The topic selection is better because it's data-informed instead of whatever caught my eye while scrolling.

Content strategy isn't about being more creative. It's about building systems that protect your creative energy for the part that actually matters.

posts.title: The career pivot nobody expected
posts.hook:
The most interesting career pivot happening right now isn't engineer to founder.

It's operator to engineer.
posts.body:
The most interesting career pivot happening right now isn't engineer to founder.

It's operator to engineer.

Sales reps learning Python. Marketers building data pipelines. RevOps people writing their own integrations instead of waiting 6 weeks for engineering to prioritize the ticket.

AI tools collapsed the learning curve. Not to zero. But from "years of CS education" to "weeks of building with Claude Code and shipping real things."

I'm watching former SDRs build better GTM automation than most dev teams because they understand the problem at a molecular level. They know which Salesforce fields are lies. They know which enrichment data actually correlates with closed-won. They know the difference between a "qualified lead" in the CRM and a qualified lead in reality.

That domain knowledge was always valuable. It just couldn't be deployed without code. Now it can.

The Arvid Kahls and Daniel Vassallos of the world proved you could leave a corporate job and ship independently. The next wave is operators who never considered themselves technical, building production systems because the tools finally met them where they are.

If you're an operator thinking about learning to build... you're not behind. You're actually ahead. You already know what to build. That's the hard part.

remixes.body:
The highest-leverage skill in 2026 isn't coding.

It's knowing what to build.

AI made writing code accessible. Cursor, Claude Code, Copilot. Someone with zero engineering background can ship a working prototype in a weekend. That's real.

But knowing what to build? That still takes domain expertise. The sales rep who builds their own lead scoring tool knows exactly what signals matter because they've worked 10,000 leads by hand. The marketer who builds a content pipeline knows what data points actually correlate with engagement because they've been staring at dashboards for years.

This is why the operator-to-engineer pivot is the most interesting thing happening in tech right now. These people aren't learning to code because they love code. They're learning to code because they're tired of waiting for someone else to build the tool they've been requesting for two years.

And the tools they build are better. Not more elegant. Not better architecture. But better at solving the actual problem, because they've lived inside that problem.

The traditional path was: learn CS, get engineering job, eventually understand the business. The new path is: understand the business deeply, pick up enough technical skill to act on that understanding directly.

Domain expertise plus basic technical ability is becoming more valuable than deep technical ability alone.
remixes.cta: What problem do you understand deeply enough to build a solution for, if the technical barrier was gone?

The people paying attention are already moving.

posts.title: Stop choosing between AI frameworks
posts.hook:
You don't need an AI agent framework.

You need a system prompt, an API key, and the discipline to ship something ugly.
posts.body:
You don't need an AI agent framework.

You need a system prompt, an API key, and the discipline to ship something ugly.

I've watched developers spend 3 weeks evaluating LangChain vs. CrewAI vs. AutoGen vs. whatever dropped on Thursday. Meanwhile, someone else wrote a 50-line script that calls Claude with a good system prompt, parses the JSON output, and pushes it to their CRM.

Guess which one is running in production.

The framework evaluation loop is procrastination wearing a technical hat. You're not doing due diligence. You're avoiding the discomfort of shipping something imperfect.

My agent system has zero frameworks. It's Python scripts. Some of them are embarrassingly simple. One of them literally reads a JSON file, sends it to Claude with instructions, and writes the response to another JSON file. It runs every night. It works.

Will it scale to 10,000 agents? No. Do I need 10,000 agents? Also no.

The pattern I keep seeing with builders who actually ship:
1. Build the dumbest possible version
2. Run it for a week
3. Fix what breaks
4. Only add complexity when you hit a real wall, not a theoretical one

Frameworks are for problems you've already solved manually and need to scale. If you haven't solved it manually yet, the framework won't save you.
posts.cta: What's the dumbest, most effective automation you're running right now?

remixes.label: shipping-velocity-proof
remixes.hook:
My most productive AI automation is 47 lines of Python.

No framework. No agents. No orchestration layer.
remixes.body:
My most productive AI automation is 47 lines of Python.

No framework. No agents. No orchestration layer.

It calls the Claude API with a system prompt, sends in raw data, gets back structured JSON, and writes it to a file. That's it. It runs every morning at 6am via a cron job and has been reliable for months.

I also have a more "sophisticated" system that I built with proper error handling, retries, logging, and modular architecture. It took three days to build. It breaks once a month because of some edge case in the abstraction layer.

The ugly 47-line script has never failed.

This is the trap I see developers falling into with AI tooling. They reach for frameworks before they've proven the concept works. They're evaluating LangChain vs. CrewAI vs. the new thing that dropped yesterday, when what they actually need is:

1. An API call to a language model
2. A good system prompt
3. JSON parsing
4. Output going somewhere useful

That's four things. You can write that in any language in under an hour. If it works, great. Now you have a working system you can iterate on. If it doesn't work, you wasted an hour instead of three weeks evaluating frameworks.

The best AI tools in production right now are embarrassingly simple. The people running them aren't talking about them because there's nothing impressive to show. Just a short script that quietly does its job.
remixes.cta: What's the simplest automation running in your workflow right now?

remixes.label: framework-graveyard
remixes.hook:
I have a folder on my desktop called "AI frameworks I evaluated."

It has 11 items in it. None of them are in production.
remixes.body:
I have a folder on my desktop called "AI frameworks I evaluated."

It has 11 items in it. None of them are in production.

You know what is in production? A collection of Python scripts that directly call the Claude API. No framework. No wrapper. No abstraction. Just system prompt, input, output, done.

Every time a new AI framework launches, the same thing happens. Developers spend days reading docs, watching tutorials, and building hello-world demos. They feel productive because they're learning. They're not productive because they're not shipping.

Frameworks solve real problems. Dependency injection, state management, retry logic. But you only need those solutions after you've proven the basic concept works. And for most AI use cases, the basic concept is: send text to an API, get text back, do something with it.

The evaluation trap is real. I've seen teams spend longer choosing between LangChain and LlamaIndex than it would have taken to build the entire feature from scratch with raw API calls.

Here's my rule now: build the ugly version first. Direct API calls, minimal error handling, hardcoded values. If it works and you need it to scale, then evaluate frameworks as a solution to a specific scaling problem. Not as a starting point.

Starting with a framework is like buying a project management tool before you have a project.

Here's my recommendation after building and shipping over a dozen AI automations: start with the simplest possible implementation. Every time.

A direct API call to Claude or GPT with a well-crafted system prompt will solve 80% of the use cases people reach for frameworks to handle. You don't need LangChain for a script that reads data, sends it to an LLM, and writes the output somewhere. You need requests, json, and 30 lines of Python.

I've seen the evaluation cycle kill more projects than bad code ever has. Teams spend weeks comparing frameworks, building proof-of-concepts with each one, debating trade-offs in architecture reviews. Meanwhile, the problem they're trying to solve sits unsolved.

My recommended approach:

First, define the exact input and output you need. What data goes in? What format comes back? Second, write a script that calls the API directly. No framework, no abstractions. Third, test it with real data. Does the output actually solve your problem? Fourth, only then, if you need retry logic, state management, or multi-step orchestration, evaluate frameworks to solve that specific need.

The frameworks aren't bad. The timing of when people adopt them is. Build the working version first. Refactor later if you need to.

You don't need an AI agent framework.

You need a system prompt, an API key, and the discipline to ship something ugly.

But you won't do that, will you? Because shipping ugly code doesn't get likes on Twitter. Evaluating the latest framework does. Writing a blog post comparing LangChain vs. CrewAI vs. AutoGen gets engagement. Writing a 50-line script that quietly makes you money doesn't.

The AI framework industry exists because developers are allergic to simplicity. We've been trained to believe that if a solution feels too simple, we're doing it wrong. So we add layers. Abstractions. Configuration files. Orchestration. Until the thing that started as a straightforward API call becomes an enterprise architecture diagram.

I watched a developer spend three weeks evaluating agent frameworks for a task that needed: call API, parse JSON, write to database. That's it. Three operations. He could have shipped it in an afternoon.

But here's the real problem. Framework evaluation feels like progress. You're reading docs, building demos, writing comparison notes. You go home feeling like you worked hard. You just didn't ship anything.

The people making real money with AI right now are running embarrassingly simple scripts. Direct API calls. Basic prompts. Cron jobs. No framework. No agents. Just code that does one thing and does it reliably.

Stop evaluating. Start shipping.

posts.title: RevOps is becoming engineering whether you like it or not
posts.hook:
RevOps job descriptions in 2024: "manage Salesforce, build reports, align sales and marketing."

RevOps job descriptions in 2026: "Python preferred, API experience required, must be comfortable with data pipelines."
posts.body:
RevOps job descriptions in 2024: "manage Salesforce, build reports, align sales and marketing."

RevOps job descriptions in 2026: "Python preferred, API experience required, must be comfortable with data pipelines."

The role didn't change. The tools did.

When your outbound motion runs through Clay --> enrichment APIs --> AI scoring --> multi-channel sequencing --> CRM sync... that's not "operations." That's engineering.

When your lead routing uses a Python script that queries intent signals from three sources, weights them against your ICP model, and pushes qualified accounts into Attio with full context... you're not operating. You're building.

The best RevOps people I know stopped asking engineering for integrations two years ago. They learned enough code to be dangerous, then used AI to close the gap between "dangerous" and "production-ready."

This isn't about replacing RevOps with engineers. It's about the role evolving to include engineering as a core skill, not a nice-to-have.

The GTM stack went from 5 tools with native integrations to 15 tools connected by APIs, webhooks, and custom logic. Someone has to build and maintain those connections. If RevOps doesn't, they become ticket-writers instead of system-builders.

The RevOps people leveling up right now will own the most strategic function in B2B within two years.
posts.cta: RevOps folks: are you learning to code, or waiting for no-code to catch up?

remixes.body:
I read 50 RevOps job listings from the last month.

The word "Python" appeared in 34 of them.

Two years ago, that number was maybe 5. The role title stayed the same. The actual job changed completely.

Think about what RevOps actually does now. You're not just managing Salesforce and building reports. You're stitching together Clay, Apollo, enrichment APIs, AI scoring models, multi-channel sequencing platforms, and CRM syncs. That's not operations. That's systems engineering with a revenue focus.

The tools forced the evolution. When your outbound stack is API-first, you need someone who can work with APIs. When your data lives across 12 platforms, you need someone who can build pipelines. When your scoring model runs on AI, you need someone who understands how to prompt and validate the output.

No-code tools tried to bridge this gap and they helped. But they hit a ceiling fast. The moment you need custom logic, conditional routing, or anything that doesn't fit a template, you're back to needing code.

The RevOps people who see this clearly are already learning. They're taking Python courses, building scripts with Claude as a co-pilot, automating the parts of their job that used to be spreadsheet gymnastics.

The ones who don't see it are going to wake up one morning wondering why the job listing for their own role requires skills they don't have.
remixes.cta: If you're in RevOps right now, what's the most technical thing you've built this year?

remixes.label: revops-identity-crisis
remixes.hook:
RevOps is having an identity crisis.

And it's the best thing that could happen to the role.
remixes.body:
RevOps is having an identity crisis.

And it's the best thing that could happen to the role.

For years, RevOps meant: own the CRM, build dashboards, make sure sales and marketing are aligned. Important work. But fundamentally administrative. You were managing tools, not building them.

That changed when the GTM stack went API-first. Clay, Apollo, enrichment services, AI scoring, multi-channel sequencing. The modern outbound motion is a data pipeline, and the person running it needs to think like an engineer.

I talk to RevOps folks every week who are in the middle of this transition. Some are thriving. They picked up Python, started building automations, and suddenly they're the most valuable person on the GTM team because they can both understand the strategy and implement it.

Others are struggling. They signed up to manage processes, not write code. And that's a fair complaint. But the market doesn't care about fair.

The opportunity is massive though. RevOps people who add technical skills aren't just keeping up. They're becoming GTM engineers. A role that barely existed two years ago and now commands significantly higher comp because the supply is tiny.

You don't need to become a software engineer. You need to be dangerous enough to build automations, connect APIs, and write scripts that eliminate the manual work choking your pipeline.

That's a much shorter learning curve than most people think.
remixes.cta: RevOps people: are you leaning into the technical shift or resisting it?

posts.title: Fully automated dev workflows are a trap
posts.hook: "Fully automated development workflows" sounds incredible until you realize the thing you automated was your judgment.
posts.body:
"Fully automated development workflows" sounds incredible until you realize the thing you automated was your judgment.

I use AI for probably 80% of my code output. Claude Code writes functions, debugs errors, scaffolds entire features. I ship 10x what I could alone.

But I review every line. Every commit message. Every architectural decision.

The push toward fully autonomous coding agents worries me. Not because the code quality is bad (it's getting shockingly good). Because the failure mode is invisible.

When a human writes bad code, they usually know it's bad. They cut corners consciously. They'll flag the tech debt.

When an AI writes bad code, it looks perfectly clean. Well-structured. Properly commented. And it might have a subtle logic error that passes every test because the tests were also generated by the same AI with the same blind spots.

The right model isn't full automation. It's aggressive augmentation with human checkpoints at the decisions that matter.

Let AI write the code. You decide what code to write.
Let AI generate tests. You decide what to test.
Let AI suggest architecture. You decide if it fits your actual constraints.

The developers winning right now aren't the ones removing themselves from the loop. They're the ones who figured out exactly which parts of the loop they're essential for.

Taste, context, and judgment. Those don't automate.
posts.cta: Where do you draw the line between AI-assisted and AI-autonomous in your workflow?

inspired_by.author: Nat Friedman
inspired_by.summary: Nat explores the intersection of AI agents and developer productivity tools, predicting a shift towards fully automated coding pipelines. He details infrastructure challenges in scaling agent swarms and shares examples from recent deployments. Calls for open-source contributions to robust AI tooling.

remixes.label: trust-but-verify-framework
remixes.hook:
I let AI write 80% of my code.

I review 100% of it.

That ratio is non-negotiable.
remixes.body:
I let AI write 80% of my code.

I review 100% of it.

That ratio is non-negotiable.

Every week I see someone post about their "fully autonomous coding workflow" where AI writes, tests, and deploys without human review. And every time, I think about the bugs I've caught in AI-generated code that would have been disasters in production.

Last month: Claude wrote a database query that worked perfectly on test data but would have created a full table scan on our production dataset. Correct output. Terrible performance. A human caught it. An automated test wouldn't have.

Two weeks ago: an AI-generated function handled the happy path flawlessly but silently swallowed errors in a way that would have made debugging impossible later. The code passed every test. It was still wrong.

These aren't AI failures. The code was syntactically correct and functionally sound in isolation. These are judgment calls that require understanding context an AI doesn't have. What does the production data look like? What does the team need to debug at 3am? What are the downstream consequences?

AI is the best coding partner I've ever had. It generates code faster than I can type, suggests approaches I wouldn't have considered, and handles boilerplate so I can focus on architecture.

But a partner is not a replacement. The moment you remove human judgment from the loop, you're not automating development. You're automating risk.
remixes.cta: What's the worst AI-generated code you caught before it shipped?

remixes.label: automation-paradox
remixes.hook:
The better AI gets at coding, the more important human developers become.

This isn't a contradiction. It's the whole point.
remixes.body:
The better AI gets at coding, the more important human developers become.

This isn't a contradiction. It's the whole point.

When AI handles 80% of code generation, the remaining 20% is all judgment calls. Architecture decisions. Security considerations. Performance trade-offs. Edge cases that only make sense if you understand the business context.

That 20% is also where all the catastrophic failures live.

A wrong variable name is a minor bug. A wrong architectural decision compounds into months of technical debt. A missed security consideration becomes a breach. A performance assumption that holds in development but collapses in production takes down your service.

AI can't evaluate these things because they require understanding beyond the code. What does the business need in six months? What's the threat model? What happened last time we made a similar choice? What does the oncall engineer need to diagnose this at 3am?

I use AI for the vast majority of my code output. I ship dramatically more than I could solo. But every line gets reviewed. Every commit message gets read. Every architectural choice gets questioned.

The push toward fully autonomous coding agents scares me. Not because the technology isn't impressive. It is. But because it optimizes for the wrong metric. Speed of code generation was never the bottleneck. Quality of decisions was.

More code, faster, with less judgment is not progress. It's a pipeline to production incidents.
remixes.cta: What decision in your codebase would you never trust AI to make alone?

tones.builder:
"Fully automated development workflows" sounds incredible until you realize the thing you automated was your judgment.

I use AI for probably 80% of my code output. Claude Code writes functions, debugs errors, scaffolds entire features. I ship 10x what I could alone.

But I review every line. Every commit message. Every architectural decision.

The push toward fully autonomous coding agents worries me. Not because the code quality is bad (it's getting shockingly good). Because the failure mode is invisible.

When a human writes bad code, they usually know it's bad. They cut corners consciously. They'll flag the tech debt.

When an AI writes bad code, it looks perfectly clean. Well-structured. Properly commented. And it might have a subtle logic error that passes every test because the tests were also generated by the same AI with the same blind spots.

The right model isn't full automation. It's aggressive augmentation with human checkpoints at the decisions that matter.

Let AI write the code. You decide what code to write.
Let AI generate tests. You decide what to test.
Let AI suggest architecture. You decide if it fits your actual constraints.

The developers winning right now aren't the ones removing themselves from the loop. They're the ones who figured out exactly which parts of the loop they're essential for.

Taste, context, and judgment. Those don't automate.
tones.advisor:
"Fully automated development workflows" sounds incredible until you realize the thing you automated was your judgment.

Here's how I think about the line between AI-assisted and AI-autonomous, and where I'd recommend you draw it.

I use AI for roughly 80% of my code output. Claude Code writes functions, debugs errors, scaffolds features. My velocity is dramatically higher than working solo. That part is straightforward.

But I review every line. Every commit. Every architectural decision. And I'd strongly recommend you do the same.

Here's my framework for what to automate and what to keep human. Automate: boilerplate, repetitive patterns, test generation, documentation, syntax-level work. Keep human: architecture decisions, security reviews, performance trade-offs, anything that requires understanding business context or production behavior.

The key distinction is reversibility. If AI makes a mistake in a utility function, you catch it in code review and fix it. Five-minute correction. If AI makes a wrong architectural decision that gets deployed, you're living with the consequences for months.

My recommendation: treat AI as a senior developer who's brilliant but just joined your team yesterday. They write great code but don't have context on your production environment, your data patterns, or your team's debugging workflows. You wouldn't let a new hire push to production without review. Apply the same standard to AI-generated code.
tones.provocateur:
"Fully automated development workflows" sounds incredible until you realize the thing you automated was your judgment.

The AI coding hype has reached a dangerous phase. People are genuinely arguing that human review of AI-generated code is an unnecessary bottleneck. That we should trust the model and ship faster.

This is insane.

I use AI for 80% of my code output. I'm not anti-AI. I'm anti-stupidity. And removing human judgment from software development is stupid, regardless of how good the models get.

Last month I caught AI-generated code that would have caused a full table scan on production data. The code was correct. The tests passed. It would have taken down the database under real load. No automated test suite in the world catches that because it requires knowing what production actually looks like.

But sure, let's talk about autonomous coding agents. Let's hand the keys to a system that doesn't know your production environment, doesn't understand your threat model, and can't be paged at 3am when the thing it deployed starts failing.

The people pushing for fully autonomous development have never been woken up by PagerDuty. They've never spent a weekend cleaning up a cascading failure caused by one bad assumption. They see code generation speed as the metric that matters.

It's not. Decision quality is. And that's still a human function.

Automate the typing. Keep the thinking.
